chemicals.concentration_notes: Cardiac glycosides are extremely potent and have a very narrow margin of safety6. Because the toxins are so powerful, there is no safe level of ingestion; even a very small amount of the plant—or simply drinking the water from a plant base or vase—can be fatal to cats23. While the toxins are present in all parts of the plant, they are highly concentrated and can cause a rapid onset of severe gastr
chemicals.description: in cats: Cardiac glycosides directly interfere with the electrical signaling and muscle contractions of the cat's heart13. They do this by inhibiting the Na + /K + -ATPase pump, which is a necessary component for normal cardiac function35. This inhibition causes an accumulation of intracellular sodium (Na + ) and extracellular potassium (K + ), which subsequently leads to a dangerous rise in intra
chemicals.name: Cardiac glycosides (specifically noted in related oleander species as cardenolides, such as oleandrin)

description: Yellow Oleander (Cascabela thevetia, formerly Thevetia peruviana) is closely related to common Oleander and equally dangerous. All parts — seeds, leaves, bark, flowers, and fruit — contain thevetin A and thevetin B, potent cardiac glycosides related to digitalis. Seeds are most concentrated and are responsible for the majority of reported poisonings.
name: Yellow Oleander
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: thevetin A and thevetin B (cardiac glycosides) in all parts; seeds most concentrated
Mechanism: thevetins inhibit Na+/K+-ATPase in cardiac muscle, disrupting electrical conduction causing arrhythmias and heart block
Symptoms: vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, severe weakness, bradycardia, cardiac arrhythmias, hypotension, collapse; often fatal without treatment
Yellow Oleander seeds are particularly accessible when the fruit falls; the decorative yellow flowers are also attractive
Any suspected ingestion is a veterinary emergency

symptoms.name: Vomiting, diarrhea, excessive drooling, and abdominal pain
symptoms.notes: Gastrointestinal distress usually acts as the initial sign of poisoning from the plant's cardiac glycosides, often appearing just prior to major heart function abnormalities34. Symptoms can persist for up to 4 to 5 days3. 2. Abnormal Heart Rate and Arrhythmias
symptoms.onset: Within the first 2 hours after exposure

symptoms.name: Bradycardia (abnormally slow heart rate) or tachycardia (rapid heart rate), and severe arrhythmias
symptoms.notes: Cardiac glycosides directly interfere with the heart by inhibiting the Na + /K + -ATPase pump, altering intracellular calcium and sodium levels [645, Conversation History]. This causes the heart's contractions to become forceful but irregular, which can rapidly lead to life-threatening cardiovascula
symptoms.onset: Expected within the first 2 hours after exposure

symptoms.name: Hyperkalemia (elevated potassium levels in the blood)
symptoms.notes: This dangerous accumulation of extracellular potassium is a direct result of the disrupted cellular pumps35. Severe hyperkalemia (> 7.5–9mEq/L) requires aggressive, emergency intravenous intervention (such as sodium bicarbonate or dextrose/insulin therapy) to stabilize the cat3. 4. Weakness and Leth
symptoms.onset: Within the first 2 hours after exposure

symptoms.name: Weakness and lethargy
symptoms.notes: The profound weakness is a systemic reflection of the severe cardiovascular disruption and plummeting blood pressure34. 5. Tremors and Seizures
symptoms.onset: Within the first 2 hours after exposure

symptoms.name: Tremors and seizures
symptoms.notes: These severe neurological manifestations occur in advanced stages of cardiac glycoside poisoning from oleander species26. 6. Difficulty Breathing
symptoms.onset: Often within the first few hours, progressing as the toxicosis worsens

symptoms.name: Respiratory changes and difficulty breathing
symptoms.notes: Respiratory distress typically accompanies the severe, systemic decline in cardiovascular output and neurological stability
symptoms.onset: Usually presents as the toxicity progresses (within hours)

treatments.description: If the cat is asymptomatic and ingested the plant recently, the veterinarian will induce vomiting to clear the stomach, followed by administering a dose of activated charcoal with a cathartic to bind unabsorbed toxins
treatments.name: Emesis Induction and Activated Charcoal
treatments.notes: Because the toxins undergo enterohepatic recirculation (recirculating between the liver and intestines), the vet will likely repeat the activated charcoal (without a cathartic) every 6 to 8 hours for a total of 3 doses to ensure the toxins are fully neutralized1. 2. Antidote Administration

treatments.description: The veterinary team administers a specific antidote originally designed for digoxin medication toxicity, which works effectively to neutralize the plant's cardiac glycosides
treatments.name: Digoxin-Specific Antibody Fragments (Fab)
treatments.notes: Patients who have access to this specific antidote generally have a guarded to good prognosis, while those without access have a guarded to poor prognosis1. 3. Intravenous Fluid Therapy and Electrolyte Management

treatments.description: The cat is given intravenous fluids to provide hemodynamic (blood flow) support while electrolytes are closely monitored
treatments.name: IV Fluid Therapy and Hyperkalemia Treatment
treatments.notes: Yellow Oleander poisoning can cause a life-threatening spike in potassium. While mild hyperkalemia can be managed with IV fluids alone, severe hyperkalemia (> 7.5–9mEq/L) requires urgent, targeted treatment using sodium bicarbonate or a dextrose/insulin therapy1. 4. Antiarrhythmic and Cardiovascular

treatments.description: The veterinarian administers specific heart medications to control the life-threatening heart rhythm abnormalities caused by the toxins
treatments.name: Pharmacological Cardiac Support (e.g., Lidocaine, Atropine, Glycopyrrolate)
treatments.notes: Antiarrhythmics like lidocaine are heavily relied upon if the digoxin-specific antidote is not available1. Additionally, if the cat is suffering from severe bradycardia (an abnormally slow heart rate), medications such as atropine or glycopyrrolate are successfully used to stabilize the heart rate
